identity.title: Coal and Other Mineral and Ore Merchant Wholesalers

lens.included_activities: US merchant wholesalers that repeatedly take title to and supply coal, coke, metal ores, ore concentrates, and nonconstruction nonmetallic minerals to external utilities, steelmakers, processors, exporters, and industrial customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Mining and beneficiation operations when not merchant-wholesale establishments; commission agents and brokers that do not take title; precious-metal bullion dealers; construction sand and gravel distributors; captive internal trading desks; shells; and firms outside the stated EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Commodity resale spreads and logistics/service margin on physical bulk materials, under a mixture of spot, indexed, bid, fixed-price, and multi-year supply arrangements, sometimes including blending, quality assurance, storage, terminal, transportation, and working-capital support.

a.bridge: No six-digit occupation table is available. BLS's combined durable-goods merchant-wholesale group includes NAICS 4235 and shows sales at 22.11% of employment, office/administrative support at 15.27%, management at 9.96%, production at 7.97%, and transportation/material moving at 26.54%. Anthropic observes AI concentrated in digital tasks and minimal use in physical-labor categories. I assign high exposure to contract comparison, bid preparation, order and document processing, customer correspondence, invoicing, credit, and market summarization; moderate exposure to sales, procurement, scheduling, quality-document review, and logistics planning; and low exposure to sampling, stockpile, terminal, rail, truck, and vessel operations. The broad BLS group may overstate standardized distribution work or understate specialized trading and regulatory analysis, creating uncertainty in both directions.
a.rationale: The information layer around contracts, prices, specifications, shipment documents, compliance, and logistics has meaningful AI exposure. Physical commodity movement, quality accountability, relationship negotiation, and exception handling remain operator-led. The provided compensation-to-receipts ratio is very low, so even a moderate exposed share applies to a small labor base.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and time study at 423520 merchants, separated into traders, customer service, contracts, compliance, laboratory/quality, logistics, terminals, and physical operations and net of existing EDI/commodity-system automation, would materially move the estimate.
caveats: The BLS occupation population spans multiple durable-goods wholesale groups and is not specific to coal and ores.
Anthropic conversations are a self-selected usage sample and do not measure labor substitution.
The code mixes office-heavy commodity merchants with physical terminal and handling operations.

rho.rationale: Within five years, firms can implement AI-assisted bid and contract review, market intelligence, customer communication, order and invoice processing, document reconciliation, logistics exception triage, and credit monitoring. Adoption is constrained by legacy commodity systems, negotiated contract nuance, volatile indexes, quality specifications, trade sanctions, transport coordination, cybersecurity, and the financial consequence of quantity or grade errors.
rho.change_evidence: Pre/post workflow data from independent coal and mineral merchants deploying AI in contract-to-cash, commodity research, trade compliance, and shipment documentation, including labor hours, error rates, disputes, and avoided hires, would materially change implementation expectations.
caveats: No controlled AI implementation study for 423520 was found.
Long-term contracts and bespoke shipping terms require human legal and commercial approval.
Small firms may lack integrated data while larger traders may already automate much of the addressable work.

e.rationale: Merchant wholesalers take title and commonly provide repeat external supply, contracting, logistics, quality, and working-capital functions. Eligibility is reduced by transactional traders without durable customer operations, firms effectively captive to one producer or buyer, project-only shipments, unusually concentrated books, and coal businesses whose customer base or reserves cannot support a transferable five-year operation.
e.change_evidence: A census of LMM 423520 firms showing repeat-customer revenue, contract tenure, commodity and end-market mix, customer and supplier concentration, owned or contracted logistics, owner dependence, and expected coal runoff would materially move eligibility.
caveats: The NAICS definition combines structurally declining US thermal coal with export metallurgical coal and diverse nonfuel minerals.
A coal producer's contracting model is only a proxy for independent merchants.
The provided firm count is margin-bridged and may include businesses with volatile EBITDA or limited service content.

s5.bridge: Gallup finds 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer within five years, while McKinsey documents an aging owner base and that closure dominates SMB exits. I discount the broad intent anchor more heavily than for ordinary distribution because environmental and reclamation exposure, commodity volatility, customer concentration, trade compliance, coal-demand uncertainty, and specialized counterparty relationships restrict qualifying buyers. The proxy may overstate completed transfers because plans fail and may understate strategic consolidation during commodity cycles.
s5.rationale: Transferable contracts, logistics relationships, licenses, credit capability, and customer books can attract buyers, but thin markets and diligence complexity make successful control transfer less likely than stated owner intent.
s5.change_evidence: Verified closed-control transactions for LMM 423520 firms from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, industry brokers, and bankruptcy or asset-sale records, matched to active eligible firms and excluding internal reorganizations, would materially move this probability.
caveats: Owner-intent and owner-age evidence is broad, not 423520-specific.
Transactions may be structured as contract, reserve, terminal, or asset purchases rather than transfers of a going merchant business.
Distress and closure are excluded from qualifying control transfers.

q.rationale: AI-enabled contract, documentation, credit, and logistics savings can remain in a merchant's spread or fixed service fee, particularly between repricing events. Retention is limited by transparent commodity indexes, formal bids, customer purchasing power, cost-plus or price-adjustment clauses, contract renewals, and competition among traders. Some benefit must be reinvested in compliance and service reliability.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level spread and fee waterfalls, price-adjustment clauses, bid history, renewal repricing, and contribution margins before and after workflow automation across coal, coke, ore, and nonmetallic-mineral merchants would materially move retention.
caveats: No public evidence directly measures pass-through of AI savings in 423520.
Retention differs sharply between indexed bulk commodity contracts and specialized minerals with quality or logistics differentiation.
Commodity-price and freight movements can overwhelm workflow savings.

d5.bridge: EIA forecast US electric-power coal consumption of 391 million short tons in 2026, a 7% annual decline, and 375 million in 2027; its AEO2026 says coal demand falls further with plant retirements and could mostly disappear from power generation if specified regulations remain. Against that, Core Natural Resources derived 56% of 2025 coal revenue from exports, including industrial and metallurgical users, while USGS reports 2025 nonfuel mineral production value rose 5.6% and mineral-reliant industries represented $4.09 trillion. I combine a declining domestic thermal-coal segment with more resilient export/metallurgical coal and diverse nonfuel ores and minerals to form a five-year constant-quality service-basket ratio. Unknown revenue weights create wide error: a thermal-heavy merchant will be lower, while a critical-mineral or metallurgical exporter may be higher.
d5.rationale: Domestic power coal faces structural retirement-driven decline, not merely a cycle. Metallurgical coal, exports, and minerals used in steel, aerospace, electronics, and industrial processes diversify the code and can sustain or grow demand, but they do not eliminate transition risk.
d5.change_evidence: 423520 revenue and physical-volume shares by thermal coal, metallurgical coal, coke, each ore, and each nonmetallic mineral, combined with contract runoffs and customer production forecasts through 2031, would materially narrow the range.
caveats: Coal consumption and mineral production are end-market proxies, not direct merchant-wholesale service volumes.
The coal forecast is highly policy-sensitive, and EIA presents alternative futures rather than a single prediction.
The code's commodity mix is unknown, making the base especially sensitive to thermal-coal weight.

o.bridge: Census defines 423520 as merchant wholesale distribution of physical coal, coke, ores, and minerals. Coal-market filings describe established customer relationships, formal bids, contracts from single shipments to multi-year volumes, renewals, export markets, transport, and quality/volume terms. I translate those physical and contractual requirements into a high continuing need for an accountable merchant operator, while allowing direct producer-to-buyer contracting, exchanges, self-service procurement, and customer vertical integration to disintermediate some volume. Producer filings may overstate direct-contract complexity relative to simpler merchant transactions and therefore may overstate operator need.
o.rationale: Software can automate search, documentation, and matching, but it cannot take title, assure grade and quantity, finance inventory, coordinate rail/terminal/vessel movements, manage counterparty exposure, or stand behind physical delivery. Declined coal demand belongs in d5; among surviving quantity, most still needs accountable operators.
o.change_evidence: Channel-share data separating producer-direct, merchant-principal, agent/broker, exchange, and vertically integrated physical volumes by commodity and customer size, plus interviews with utility, steel, and industrial procurement leaders, would materially move this share.
caveats: Operator requirement is distinct from the shrinking quantity of thermal coal demand.
Direct long-term contracts can bypass independent merchants, especially for large utilities and steelmakers.
Digital marketplaces may reduce intermediation in standardized spot trades but still rely on physical logistics and accountable counterparties.

narrative.executive_view: The code contains a modest AI opportunity in document-, contract-, and market-heavy workflows, but its very low labor share limits the economic magnitude. Physical and counterparty accountability remain durable, while domestic thermal-coal exposure creates a material demand overhang that cannot be solved with workflow automation.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can compare contracts, prepare bids, summarize markets, reconcile shipment and quality documents, draft customer updates, support compliance, and prioritize credit or logistics exceptions. Human traders, contract owners, quality personnel, and logistics operators remain accountable for prices, grades, quantities, sanctions, transport, and delivery.
narrative.value_capture: Merchants may retain savings in spreads and fees between bid or renewal events, but transparent indexes, large customers, cost and price reopeners, and commodity competition drive sharing. Savings are small relative to the underlying commodity and freight value at risk.
narrative.firm_availability: Repeat external contracts and logistics relationships can transfer, but commodity concentration, environmental diligence, customer dependence, and coal transition risk narrow both eligibility and the buyer universe. Generic small-business succession intent is therefore discounted.
narrative.demand_durability: US power-sector coal consumption is forecast to decline and remains highly policy-sensitive. Export and metallurgical coal plus nonfuel ores and critical minerals diversify the basket, producing a wide five-year range rather than a uniform decline assumption.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The unknown mix of thermal coal, metallurgical coal, coke, and dozens of minerals is the central evidence gap. Broad occupation data, producer rather than merchant contracting evidence, policy-sensitive coal forecasts, sanctions, commodity and freight volatility, credit exposure, and potential direct contracting all weaken precision.
narrative.principal_driver: Complex contracts, shipment documents, market intelligence, and logistics coordination offer repeatable AI-assisted workflows around physical commodities.
narrative.principal_weakness: Domestic thermal-coal decline and an extremely low labor-to-receipts ratio limit the benefit, while the code's commodity mix is opaque.

sources.title: NAICS definition: 423520 Coal and Other Mineral and Ore Merchant Wholesalers
sources.supports: Defines the industry as merchant wholesale distribution of coal, coke, metal ores, and nonmetallic minerals, excluding precious and semiprecious stones and construction minerals such as sand and gravel.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Merchant Wholesalers, Durable Goods, selected groups including NAICS 4235
sources.supports: Reports the broad occupation mix including sales 22.11%, office/administrative support 15.27%, management 9.96%, production 7.97%, and transportation/material moving 26.54%.

sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index: Mapping AI usage across occupational tasks
sources.supports: Finds AI usage concentrated in digital tasks, physical-labor categories least represented, moderate task-level diffusion across occupations, and more augmentation than automation in observed conversations.

sources.title: Core Natural Resources, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes coal sales to established customers through relationships and bids, contracts from single shipments to multi-year volumes, renewal practices, approximately 110 customers, and 56% of 2025 coal revenue from export markets.

sources.title: Peabody Energy Corporation 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes competitive bidding and negotiated long-term coal supply agreements with price adjustments, quality requirements, quantity parameters, environmental constraints, extensions, and termination provisions.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall-2024 survey reports 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer within five years and 17% of owners age 55 or older planned a sale or transfer.

sources.title: McKinsey: Navigating the great small business ownership transition
sources.supports: Estimates more than half of US small-business owners are over 55 and reports closure dominates observed SMB exits, indicating execution risk beyond stated transfer plans.

sources.title: EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook, February 2026: Coal markets
sources.supports: Forecasts US electric-power coal consumption at 391 million short tons in 2026, down 7% year over year, and a further decline to 375 million short tons in 2027.

sources.title: USGS: Value of U.S. mineral production rose in 2025
sources.supports: Reports overall US mineral production value rose 5.6% to $112 billion in 2025 and mineral-reliant industries represented $4.09 trillion, while documenting the breadth and importance of nonfuel minerals.

sources.title: EIA releases the Annual Energy Outlook 2026
sources.supports: States coal plant retirements drive US coal demand lower and that coal generation could mostly disappear from the power sector if the specified 2024 emissions regulations are enforced; emphasizes that AEO cases are alternative futures, not predictions.
